stewart_read: The rep calls the customer 'Raymond' in the opening question, then immediately introduces himself as 'Randy' — his own name. Per the codex, legitimacy in the first 30 seconds is a primary success signal. A name confusion at the intro, even minor, introduces a small but real friction: the customer has to parse whether the rep knows who he's calling. Raymond's 'Yeah. Who's speaking?' at 00:10 suggests he was already slightly uncertain. The rep recovered quickly, but the opener was not clean.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep simply introduced himself by his own name after confirming the customer's — a normal sequence that Raymond processed without issue.
coaching_implication: Low-stakes but worth a single rep note: confirm the customer's name, pause for their response, then introduce yourself. The sequence matters for legitimacy. Not a coaching priority on its own, but worth flagging if this rep shows a pattern of rushed intros.

quote: There's actually no upfront or out of pocket cost whatsoever.
stewart_read: The rep volunteers 'no upfront or out of pocket cost whatsoever' unprompted, before Raymond has raised any cost concern. Per the codex's curiosity_over_features anti-pattern, proactively selling features — especially cost claims — creates objections rather than curiosity. Raymond's response at 03:47 ('some people get them for free. I don't believe that') is a direct downstream consequence: the rep's 'free' framing seeded the skepticism it then failed to resolve. The rep did not deploy an escape hatch or a flip — instead offered a vague 'it really just depends' before pivoting to the bill-swap reframe.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep was following the program-pitch beat and the cost framing is standard setter language — the skepticism may have emerged regardless.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the 02:24 → 03:47 sequence together. The question to ask in the 1-on-1: 'What did you say that made him say he didn't believe it?' The rep needs to see that proactive cost claims invite disbelief on a customer who hasn't asked. The fix is not to remove the cost framing — it's to deploy it in response to a cost question, not ahead of one.

quote: Oh, I think we just had somebody come out, and they said we got another ten ten years left. So I'm gonna assume twenty years.
stewart_read: Raymond's answer signals a roof that is approximately 10–15 years old already (inspector says 10 years left; Raymond estimates 20 years total age). The codex hard-DQ threshold is roof_age_over_15_no_replacement_planned. The rep accepted the estimate without probing — no follow-up on whether Raymond has a roof replacement planned or budgeted. This is simultaneously a qualification risk and a missed cross-sell signal: if the roof is approaching end-of-life, Ion's in-house roofing team is the correct route, and a roof-plus-solar conversation may be more valuable than a solar-only appointment. The rep's silence here is a reactive miss.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have assessed 20 years as within acceptable range and moved on deliberately — a reasonable read if the threshold is interpreted as 'over 15 years old' rather than 'under 10 years remaining.'
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the dq_rules threshold and the cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned bridge. The coaching question: 'What would you have done if he said 12 years left?' The rep needs to know that a roof nearing end-of-life is not a disqualifier — it's a routing signal to the roofing team, and potentially a stronger lead than a solar-only conversation.

quote: That's not my department. This is my wife.
stewart_read: Raymond explicitly names his wife as the person who manages the bills at 03:32 — and she is already confirmed on the title at 02:10. This is a double signal: the wife is both a co-decision-maker and the gatekeeper for the bill the rep needs. The rep's response is a social laugh-off ('Oh, yeah. Yeah.') with no move to include the wife in the appointment. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct response is to include the spouse in the appointment — not to overcome the mention as an objection, but to route the appointment to include her. The rep instead defers to 'get with your wife' at 06:33, which leaves both the bill and the decision-maker outside the appointment structure.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have read the wife mention as a bill-logistics comment rather than a decision-maker signal, and planned to address spouse inclusion at button-up.
coaching_implication: This is the call's most consequential structural miss. The manager should replay 02:10 and 03:32 together: wife is on the title, wife manages the bills, wife was never included. The coaching move is not 'you should have included the wife' in the abstract — it's 'at 03:32, when he said it's his wife's department, that was your cue to say: let's make sure she's on the call with us. What time works for both of you?' One sentence at that moment changes the appointment's structural integrity.

quote: Yeah. Yeah. So it really just depends. I mean, different companies have, you know, different programs. So I'm really not sure, you know, what kind of deals they got or what the programs they were offering.
stewart_read: When Raymond raises cost skepticism, the rep opens with a vague non-answer ('I'm really not sure') before pivoting to the bill-swap reframe at 04:15. The pivot itself is correct setter-level content. The failure is the opener: the rep doesn't deploy the escape hatch ('that's exactly the kind of question our specialist handles') and doesn't deploy an objection inversion ('what's expensive is your current bill'). The rep knows the bill-swap frame — the destination is right — but the path through the vague opener signals the rep doesn't yet have a clean trigger for when to use the escape hatch versus the reframe. This is a mixed-intent moment, not a pure reactive failure.
alternative_hypothesis: The vague opener was a deliberate softener before the reframe — a pacing move rather than a knowledge gap.
coaching_implication: The rep has the bill-swap reframe in their toolkit — that's the good news. The coaching gap is the trigger: when a customer raises cost skepticism, the rep needs a clean entry point rather than a vague opener. Suggested focus for the 1-on-1: 'You got to the right place at 04:15 — the bill-swap frame worked. What would it look like to get there in one sentence instead of four?' Also surface the flip opportunity: Raymond's 'really, really expensive' at 03:57 was an inversion setup the rep walked past.

quote: get with your wife and see if you can just, take a picture of of, your bill and send it over, then I can get you guys on the on the schedule here and have that design put together for you.
stewart_read: The rep closes the call with an explicit condition: the appointment is not confirmed until the bill arrives. This is a conditional booking, not a firm sit. The rep had three prior opportunities to resolve the bill-collection path (03:25, 03:32, and implicitly throughout) and deferred each time. The button_up protocol requires appointment datetime confirmed, one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving-at-computer anchor, and 'is that fair' handshake — none of these were completed. The conditional close also means the rep's compensation is at risk: setters are paid on sits, not bookings, and a bill that never arrives means no sit.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have assessed that pushing for a verbal readback would have felt intrusive given Raymond's 'not my department' framing, and chose to preserve rapport by deferring to the wife.
coaching_implication: Connect this moment directly to the rep's paycheck: 'If Raymond doesn't send the bill tonight, does this sit happen?' Walk through the bill_collection.alternative_paths options — verbal readback, defer-to-specialist — and ask the rep which one they would use next time. The button_up checklist is also worth reviewing: the rep confirmed a time but skipped the handshake, the computer anchor, and the one-hour block statement.

quote: I think it's, like, about 50 a month.
stewart_read: Raymond's $50/month bill is the call's primary anchor opportunity. The rep captures it (with a brief mishear at 02:50 — '$1.50?' — that Raymond corrects) and then moves directly to the prior-designs question at 02:56. No emotional flip, no alignment-against-utility, no acknowledgment of what $50 means in the context of solar savings. Per the codex, bill capture alone is necessary but not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment. At $50/month, the flip is admittedly harder than a $300 bill, but the opportunity was still present: 'So you're paying ComEd $600 a year — and that number only goes up. That's exactly what we're looking to replace.'
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have assessed $50/month as a low-pain anchor and deliberately moved on, planning to let the specialist handle the savings conversation.
coaching_implication: Surface the bill_anchoring failure pattern: 'ask + acknowledge + move on' versus 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' Even a low bill has a flip available — the utility's rate trajectory, not the current number, is the emotional lever. This is a floor-wide coaching priority per the codex, and this call is a clean example of the universal failure mode.
